topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. My stomach clenched instantly, a tight, unpleasant knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling slightly as I scanned the subject line. It was a notification about an article I had poured months of my life into—my research, my unique perspective—and the author listed was not me. A wave of hot frustration washed over me, making my cheeks flush. I reread the attribution, my eyes darting back and forth across the screen, searching for a mistake, a typo, anything to rationalize this monumental oversight. When I finally found the confirmation—a completely different name attached to my work—a sharp, bitter taste flooded my mouth. I slammed my laptop shut, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet apartment. I stood up, pacing the small space, my jaw tight, every muscle in my body vibrating with a simmering resentment.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen until the pixels blurred. The realization hit me with the sudden, shocking force of a physical impact. My breath hitched, catching painfully in my throat. This wasn't just an error; this felt like a profound erasure. I felt a deep, cold settling in my chest, a heavy weight that made breathing shallow. I started shaking my head, a jerky, involuntary motion, trying to dislodge the unbelievable reality. When I finally managed to type a response, my hands were slick with sweat. The words I composed were clipped, sharp, laced with an unmistakable edge of deep displeasure. I reread them three times, my gaze fixed on the screen, ensuring every syllable conveyed the sheer magnitude of the disrespect I was experiencing. The silence in the room was thick, charged with the unspoken disappointment radiating off me.

[story 3]

When the notification popped up, I froze mid-sip of my coffee. The lukewarm liquid suddenly felt gritty in my throat. My vision tunneled for a second, the bright light of the monitor seeming too harsh. Slowly, deliberately, I brought my hand up to grip the edge of the desk, my knuckles turning white. A slow, burning heat began to spread from my chest outwards, radiating into my arms. I leaned back in my chair, the movement stiff and jerky, and let out a low, almost inaudible sound of pure disbelief. I didn't move for a long moment, just absorbing the injustice. Then, I started typing, my movements precise but fueled by a simmering, contained fury. Each character I struck felt heavy, weighted with the feeling that someone had completely disregarded my contribution.
